title: Reinforcement Learning
blurb: ★ Overview only · Reinforcement learning frames sequential decision-making as a Markov Decision Process where an agent maximizes long-term discounted reward. This chapter builds from MDPs and Bellman equations through model-free control (Monte Carlo, TD, Q-learning) up to Deep Q-Networks with experience replay and target networks.

knowledge_points.title: MDPs, Returns & Bellman Equations
knowledge_points.recap: An **MDP** $(\mathcal{S},\mathcal{A},\mathcal{P},\mathcal{R},\gamma)$ formalizes the environment; a state is **Markov** if the future depends only on the present. The agent maximizes the **discounted return** $G_t=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ with **discount factor** $\gamma\in[0,1]$. The **state-value** $v_\pi(s)$ and **action-value** $q_\pi(s,a)$ measure long-term value under policy $\pi$. The **Bellman expectation equation** decomposes value into immediate reward plus discounted next-state value (linear, for a fixed policy); the **Bellman optimality equation** replaces the average over actions with a **max** (non-linear, for control).

questions.q: State the Markov property and explain the difference between the Bellman expectation equation and the Bellman optimality equation.
questions.answer: The **Markov property** says the future depends only on the present state, not the past history: the current state already encodes everything relevant for predicting the next state. The **Bellman expectation equation** expresses the value of a **fixed policy** as immediate reward plus the (discounted) expected value of the next state, **averaging over actions according to that policy**; it is **linear** and has a closed-form matrix solution. The **Bellman optimality equation** instead takes the **max over actions** rather than averaging, describing the best achievable value $v^*(s)$ / $q^*(s,a)$; it is **non-linear** with no general closed form and is solved iteratively (value iteration, policy iteration, Q-learning, SARSA). Knowing $q^*(s,a)$ essentially solves the MDP: in each state pick the action with the largest action-value.
questions.extend: Prediction (policy evaluation) estimates the value of a fixed policy; control finds the optimal policy by combining evaluation with greedy policy improvement.

questions.q: An agent receives rewards $R_1=1, R_2=1, R_3=1, R_4=1$ over four steps with discount factor $\gamma=0.9$ and zero reward thereafter. Compute the discounted return $G_0$.
questions.extend: Small $\gamma$ produces short-sighted behavior; large $\gamma$ favors long-horizon reward. The discount also keeps the infinite sum finite.

questions.q: In the gridworld S1 - S2 - Goal, each move costs -1 and reaching the Goal from S2 gives +10. Starting value iteration from V=0 everywhere, compute the iteration-1 value of S2 (best action).
questions.answer: From S2 the agent compares actions: going **left** yields $-1$ (cost, next state value still 0), going **right** to the Goal yields $-1+10=+9$. Value iteration takes the **max**, so $V(S2)=+9$ after iteration 1.
questions.extend: At iteration 2, S1's best value becomes $-1+9=+8$ by bootstrapping from the improved V(S2).

knowledge_points.title: Model-Free Prediction & Control
knowledge_points.recap: When the model is unknown the agent learns from sampled experience. **Monte Carlo** waits for a complete episode and updates toward the realized return (unbiased, high variance). **Temporal-Difference** learning **bootstraps**, updating from the immediate reward plus the estimated next-state value (biased but often faster, lower variance). Control needs both good value estimates and **exploration** via **epsilon-greedy**. **SARSA** is **on-policy** (learns the policy it follows, including exploratory actions); **Q-learning** is **off-policy** (learns the greedy policy while following another).

questions.q: Contrast Monte Carlo and Temporal-Difference prediction, including the meaning of bootstrapping and the bias-variance trade-off.
questions.answer: **Monte Carlo (MC)** learns from the 'finish line': it waits until an episode terminates, observes the **actual realized return**, and moves the value estimate toward it (value = mean return). It needs no model but usually requires episodes to terminate and can be slow. **Temporal-Difference (TD)** learns 'step-by-step': it **bootstraps**, updating an estimate using another estimate — namely the **immediate reward plus the estimated value of the next state** (TD(0) target $R_{t+1}+\gamma V(S_{t+1})$), and the **TD error** measures how surprising the one-step outcome was. Trade-off: **MC has low/no bias but high variance**, while **TD has more bias (an estimate of an estimate) but lower variance and is often more data-efficient / online**.
questions.extend: MC and TD form a continuum; intermediate methods like n-step returns or TD(lambda) blend them.

questions.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes Q-learning from SARSA?
questions.answer: Q-learning is off-policy (learns the greedy target policy while following an exploratory behavior policy); SARSA is on-policy.

options.text: Q-learning is off-policy: it learns about the greedy policy while following a different (exploratory) policy; SARSA is on-policy.
SARSA is off-policy and Q-learning is on-policy.
Both are model-based dynamic-programming methods requiring the transition matrix.
Q-learning requires complete episodes while SARSA bootstraps.

questions.extend: Off-policy learning is more sample-efficient because it can reuse data from other/older/exploratory policies.

questions.q: Why is greedy improvement alone insufficient in model-free control, and how does epsilon-greedy address it?
questions.answer: If the agent always exploits (picks what currently looks best), it may **never discover better alternatives** because unseen actions never get tried — this is the **exploration vs. exploitation** dilemma. **Epsilon-greedy** balances the two: with probability $1-\epsilon$ take the current greedy action (exploit), and with probability $\epsilon$ take a random action (explore). This guarantees continued exploration so the agent can improve its value estimates for all actions.
questions.extend: More advanced strategies include bandit methods and GLIE schedules that decay epsilon over time.

knowledge_points.title: Deep RL & DQN
knowledge_points.recap: Tabular RL stores one value per state-action pair and breaks in large/continuous spaces, so modern RL uses **function approximation** (neural networks) that **generalize** across similar states. This bridge to **Deep RL** is unstable because bootstrapping + off-policy + function approximation can diverge. **DQN** stabilizes training with two tools: **experience replay** (store past transitions and sample them to break temporal correlations and reuse data) and a **target network** (a slowly-updated copy that provides stable targets instead of chasing a moving target).

questions.q: Explain why naive neural-network Q-learning is unstable and how experience replay and target networks help.
questions.answer: Combining **bootstrapping, off-policy learning and function approximation** can make training **unstable/diverge**. Two problems: online transitions are **strongly temporally correlated**, and the TD **target depends on the network's own current weights**, so as the network updates, the target it chases keeps moving (like a dog chasing its tail), causing oscillations. **Experience replay** stores past transitions in a buffer and **samples them randomly later**, breaking temporal correlations and using data more efficiently. A **target network** is a periodically-updated copy of the Q-network used to compute the targets, so the target stays fixed for a while and training is far more stable.
questions.extend: DeepMind's 2013 DQN combined these tricks to learn control directly from high-dimensional inputs.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit): DQN regression-target construction from a replay batch (CartPole).
questions.extend: CartPole has a 4-D state [cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, pole angular velocity], two actions (+1/-1 force), reward +1 per upright step, gamma=0.95, with epsilon decayed over time.

blanks.hint: terminal target is just the immediate reward

variants.code:
```python
q_next = np.____(1)(target_next[i])   # best next-state value
target[i][action[i]] = reward[i] + ____(2) * q_next
```

questions.q: Why does modern RL replace lookup tables with parameterized function approximators?
questions.answer: Tables need one value per state-action pair and cannot scale to large/continuous spaces; approximators generalize across similar states.

options.text: Tabular storage breaks in large or continuous spaces (e.g. Go, robotics); approximators let similar states share information and generalize.
Function approximators remove the need for a reward signal.
They make the environment fully known so dynamic programming applies.
They guarantee convergence without any additional engineering.

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style concept questions in the professor's format. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q: (a) State the **Markov property**. (b) Explain the difference between the **Bellman expectation equation** and the **Bellman optimality equation**, and say why one is linear while the other is not.
questions.answer: (a) The **Markov property** states that the future depends only on the **current state**, not on the past history — the current state already encodes all information relevant for predicting the next state and reward. (b) The **Bellman expectation equation** gives the value of a **fixed policy** as immediate reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state, **averaging over actions according to that policy**; because it is an average with fixed weights it is **linear** and has a closed-form (matrix) solution. The **Bellman optimality equation** takes the **max over actions** instead of averaging, describing the best achievable value $v^*(s)/q^*(s,a)$; the max makes it **non-linear** with no general closed form, so it is solved iteratively (value/policy iteration, Q-learning, SARSA).
questions.extend: Knowing $q^*(s,a)$ essentially solves the MDP: in each state just pick the action with the largest action-value.

questions.q: (a) Explain the **exploration vs. exploitation** dilemma and how **epsilon-greedy** resolves it. (b) Briefly contrast **Monte Carlo** and **Temporal-Difference** learning, including what **bootstrapping** means.
questions.answer: (a) A purely greedy agent always exploits what currently looks best and may **never try — and therefore never discover — better actions**; this tension between using known good actions and trying new ones is the **exploration vs. exploitation** dilemma. **Epsilon-greedy** resolves it by taking the greedy action with probability $1-\epsilon$ (exploit) and a random action with probability $\epsilon$ (explore), guaranteeing continued exploration. (b) **Monte Carlo** waits for a **complete episode** and updates toward the actual realized return — **unbiased but high variance**. **Temporal-Difference** **bootstraps**: it updates an estimate using another estimate, namely immediate reward plus the discounted estimated value of the next state ($R_{t+1}+\gamma V(S_{t+1})$) — **biased (an estimate of an estimate) but lower variance and works online**.
questions.extend: MC and TD form a continuum; n-step returns and TD(lambda) blend them. Epsilon is often decayed over time (GLIE).

questions.q: A **DQN** adds **experience replay** and a **target network** to neural-network Q-learning. Which statements about why these are needed are correct?
questions.answer: Experience replay breaks temporal correlation and reuses data; the target network provides a stable, slowly-updated target; both address instability from combining bootstrapping, off-policy learning and function approximation.

options.text: Experience replay stores past transitions and samples them randomly, breaking temporal correlations and improving data efficiency.
A target network is a periodically-updated copy of the Q-network that supplies stable targets instead of a constantly moving one.
Naive NN Q-learning is unstable because bootstrapping, off-policy learning and function approximation together can diverge.
Experience replay is needed because tabular Q-learning cannot represent continuous rewards.

questions.extend: Q-learning is off-policy (learns the greedy policy while following an exploratory one); SARSA is on-policy.
